jokes.text: Jaime's fake-out: 'I failed the test... and I'm getting deported' — followed by 'Just kidding! I passed!'
jokes.setup: Solemn horn music, cheering crowd, Jaime arrives looking devastated and announces he failed his citizenship test and is being deported
jokes.punchline: It was all a prank — he passed. The whole emotional setup was a misdirect.
jokes.explanation: Solid cold-open misdirection. The patriotic music puncturing into apparent tragedy and then immediately reversing is a well-executed structural fake-out. Not particularly original but executed with good timing and earns the gasps from Abbi and Ilana. The emotional whiplash is the joke.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana gasp simultaneously and in unison at Jaime's fake bad news
jokes.setup: Jaime delivers devastating news about deportation
jokes.punchline: Both women react with perfectly synchronized over-the-top gasps, playing it completely straight
jokes.explanation: The synchronized gasp is a visual gag embedded in their friendship shorthand. Works as a beat but is secondary to the larger fake-out joke around it.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Jaime, those old white dudes are impossible to tell apart.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is trying to console Jaime about failing the citizenship test by identifying the faces of the Founding Fathers
jokes.punchline: She flips the usual racial trope — instead of POC being 'hard to tell apart,' it's the old white men on the test. Delivered with complete sincerity as comfort.
jokes.explanation: Sharp and Ilana-specific — the inversion of a racist trope delivered as earnest comfort is quintessential Ilana Glazer writing. The economy is excellent: one sentence does the setup, subversion, and character work simultaneously. Could only come from Ilana. Borderline 8 on craft; held back slightly because the subversion is fairly well-trodden as a comedic device, even if the specific application is good.

jokes.text: Abbi's flat 'Yeah... mine didn't' in response to Ilana's speech about ancestors passing through Ellis Island
jokes.setup: Ilana is excitedly romanticizing how 'our ancestors passed through Ellis Island' while on the citizenship boat cruise
jokes.punchline: Abbi quietly, flatly deflates the whole speech by acknowledging that Black American ancestors did not come through Ellis Island willingly — if at all
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Five words ('Yeah... mine didn't.') do an enormous amount of work: they puncture Ilana's well-meaning but oblivious speech, they reference the Middle Passage without saying it, they land as both a comedic beat and a genuine historical corrective, and the beat of silence before Ilana's 'Right. Right, right, right.' makes it land harder. The economy is nearly perfect. The pause structure (the [01:03] timestamp gap) confirms this is meant to sit in silence. Dark, smart, earned.

jokes.text: Ilana's flustered 'Right. Right, right, right.' after Abbi's Ellis Island comment — clearly covering for a social blunder
jokes.setup: Ilana just romantically included Abbi in the Ellis Island immigrant narrative
jokes.punchline: Abbi's correction leaves Ilana visibly scrambling; her rapid-fire 'rights' are a verbal performance of someone trying to recover from accidentally being oblivious
jokes.explanation: The punchline to joke 4. Ilana's overcorrection is both funny and character-accurate: she's someone who prides herself on wokeness and is mortified to have steamrolled Abbi. The triple 'right' is a great piece of verbal slapstick.

jokes.text: Ilana pre-emptively distributes Ziploc bags to smuggle free food and booze off the boat: 'I brought ziplocs, pass it down.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is describing an exclusive law firm boat party they're attending as guests
jokes.punchline: Without breaking stride, she reveals she has already prepared food-theft infrastructure, treating a fancy corporate event as a personal buffet heist
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Ilana: the casual pre-meditation is funnier than any elaborate scheme would be. She doesn't announce a plan, she just executes it, which makes it entirely in-character. 'Pass it down' is a beautiful throwaway that implies she's already thought through the logistics. High character integration — this is Ilana's whole worldview expressed in four words.

jokes.text: Abbi introduces herself to the boat list-checker as 'Hello, I am Abbi Abrams, thank you' — overly formal, already performing
jokes.setup: Abbi approaches a velvet-rope-style boat check-in, nervous about whether she's on the list
jokes.punchline: She leads with 'Hello, I am Abbi Abrams, thank you' — awkwardly formal, thanking him for nothing yet, telegraphing her social anxiety through hyper-politeness
jokes.explanation: Small but accurate character beat. The 'thank you' before being helped is a perfect Abbi nervous-people-pleaser tell. Competent character comedy that rewards familiarity with Abbi.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'How are you possibly reading all the names? You're going so fast.' — as the bouncer rapidly scans his list
jokes.setup: Abbi is nervous her name isn't on the list and fills the silence with an absurd observation about the bouncer's reading speed
jokes.punchline: Instead of standing confidently, she compliments/challenges his list-reading prowess, revealing her complete inability to handle social gatekeeping without oversharing
jokes.explanation: Another Abbi-specific cringe beat — her anxiety manifests as weirdly impressed commentary on the bouncer's scanning. Functional character comedy, not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: Abbi pretends to have an assistant named 'Chrissy' and loudly berates her in front of the bouncer to explain why her plus-three wasn't confirmed
jokes.setup: Abbi is not on the list as plus-three and needs to improvise an explanation. She invents a fictional assistant.
jokes.punchline: She launches into a full performance of a powerful person with a flaky assistant, threatening to fire Chrissy, then pivoting to sympathy ('your mom died two weeks ago, Chrissy!') before snapping back to fury — all to an imaginary person on an imaginary phone
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best sustained cringe bits. The escalation from fake call to fake sympathy ('her mom died two weeks ago, Chrissy!') to 'get your shit together' is perfectly structured. It's fundamentally about how far Abbi will go in public to avoid admitting she doesn't belong somewhere, which is core character. The timestamps show the performance playing out in real time with pauses — the silences where we watch her pretend to listen are the joke's skeleton. The 'your mom died' beat is the peak and a genuinely surprising escalation.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Just please don't hit me in front of everybody.' Then immediately: 'Your mom died two weeks ago, Chrissy! Get your shit together!'
jokes.setup: Abbi is performing an increasingly elaborate fake phone fight to get on the boat
jokes.punchline: She pivots from begging not to be hit (establishing fictional abuse dynamic) to cruelly invoking Chrissy's dead mother — weaponizing grief in a fake argument
jokes.explanation: The escalation to dead-mom territory is the surprise that makes this sequence memorable. The structure is tight: she first establishes sympathy ('don't hit me') then immediately pivots to cruelty ('your mom died'), which is a perfect comic reversal. The specificity of 'two weeks ago' makes it funnier — Abbi has somehow constructed a fully detailed fake tragedy mid-scheme. This earns its high score through structural excellence and escalation payoff.

jokes.text: Abbi sadly waves goodbye to the balloons that can't come on the boat: 'Bye-bye, balloons.'
jokes.setup: After the dramatic fake-phone-call ordeal to get on the boat, Abbi must leave celebration balloons behind at the dock
jokes.punchline: Her defeated little 'bye-bye, balloons' — treating the loss of the balloons with genuine quiet grief after everything she just went through
jokes.explanation: Efficient, character-driven beat. The emotional deflation from the high of getting on the boat to the quiet mourning of the balloons is classically Abbi. Economy is strong — three words do the whole joke. Genuinely sweet and funny.

jokes.text: Man at the party describes his daughter's age as '11-quarters old' after discussing business in quarters
jokes.setup: A lawyer on the boat is speaking in corporate quarterly language
jokes.punchline: He's so deep in finance-speak that he involuntarily calculates his baby's age in fiscal quarters
jokes.explanation: Tight observational joke about Wall Street/law firm culture leaking into personal life. The 'born in Q-3 of '012' formulation is a specific, well-constructed piece of absurdism. Works as a quick world-building joke about the boat's milieu.

jokes.text: Lincoln: 'I bet every guy on this boat is into annas with bananas' / 'Denises the penises. Gals with dongs, bro.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln is speculating about the sexual preferences of the lawyers on the boat
jokes.punchline: He invents two rhyming slang terms for trans women ('annas with bananas,' 'Denises the penises') delivered with the confidence of established vernacular
jokes.explanation: The rhyming coinages are genuinely inventive wordplay and Lincoln's delivery of them as obvious common knowledge is the joke's engine. 'Denises the penises' is the stronger construction. Slightly dated in its framing now but the wordplay craft is real. Earned vs. cheap score reflects some tension in how the joke positions trans women as a punchline.

jokes.text: Jaime flatly: 'You say that about every single white-collar professional.' — calling out Lincoln's pattern
jokes.setup: Lincoln confidently asserts 'lawyers love' trans women, as if this is a known fact
jokes.punchline: Jaime immediately punctures it by revealing this is a recurring Lincoln claim he applies to every profession — no specificity, just a running fantasy Lincoln projects onto any room of professionals
jokes.explanation: Quick efficient comedy — the callback/retort lands because it instantly contextualizes Lincoln as someone who enthusiastically makes this claim in every setting. One line of characterization through Jaime's patient exasperation.

jokes.text: Two Murray Hill guys say 'Murray Hill' in perfect unison, then one adds 'But I'm moving to Williamsburg!' — beat of silence follows
jokes.setup: Abbi asks where the two interchangeable-looking guys live
jokes.punchline: They answer in unison ('Murray Hill') then one tries to differentiate himself with the Williamsburg announcement, which lands in silence — the ultimate NYC social signifier joke
jokes.explanation: Sharp NYC-specific joke. The synchronized 'Murray Hill' and then one guy's sad try at distinction ('But I'm moving to Williamsburg!') perfectly skewers a very specific type of young finance bro trying to seem cooler than he is. The implied beat of silence after it lands makes it funnier. Economical and site-specific.

jokes.text: Jaime orders a 'gin and vodka maraschino sizzle... with curacao, and a little splash of crème de menthe' — completely making up a drink
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana offer to get Jaime whatever he wants from the bar on his big night
jokes.punchline: Jaime orders with the specificity and confidence of a cocktail expert but the drink is pure nonsense — gin AND vodka, maraschino 'sizzle,' crème de menthe — a genuinely undrinkable fantasy
jokes.explanation: Charming character comedy — Jaime performs American consumer confidence by ordering a ludicrously specific fake cocktail. His eagerness and the specificity of the nonsense words makes it work. The beat where Abbi just says 'I'll have one, too' without questioning it is the cap.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'They are totally getting beer.' — as soon as Jaime is out of earshot
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana have just committed to getting Jaime his elaborate fake cocktail
jokes.punchline: The moment he's gone, Ilana instantly drops the pretense — they were never going to attempt that drink
jokes.explanation: Perfect button on the Jaime drink-order joke. The immediacy and certainty of Ilana's delivery make it land. Economy is nearly perfect — it's the minimum words needed to collapse the whole pretense.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'She just went to the can, she had to take a doody.'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana ask where Melody is
jokes.punchline: Bevers overshares with absolute comfort, using baby-talk ('doody') to describe his girlfriend's bathroom activities to near-strangers at a formal law firm event
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Bevers — the use of 'doody' in a formal setting without any self-awareness is perfect character work. The casual oversharing of Melody's bathroom activities is exactly the energy that makes Abbi's life a nightmare. High character integration score.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I'm sure she'll be so happy you told us.' — after Bevers announces Melody is taking a doody
jokes.setup: Bevers has just announced Melody's bowel situation
jokes.punchline: Abbi's dry, completely flat response treats Bevers' overshare as an obvious social failure without bothering to explain why
jokes.explanation: Competent straight-man beat. Abbi as exasperated Bevers-reactor is well-trodden territory for this show, but the line reading would elevate it in performance. Functional.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'I've sailed on this boat with Melody for 12 quarters now. I know all its secrets. The captain is a murderer.'
jokes.setup: Bevers is trying to establish his credentials as a boat expert to convince Abbi and Ilana to follow him to the secret liquor
jokes.punchline: After two normal facts, he casually drops 'The captain is a murderer' as if it's equivalent information — then walks away as though that's resolved
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structure is nearly perfect: establish-establish-detonate. Bevers' complete casual confidence in the murderer claim, delivered with no more weight than the previous boat facts, is the joke. The fact that it's never followed up on makes it funnier — it's not a setup for anything, it's just Bevers' relationship with reality. Character-specific: this only works because we already know Bevers operates on a different plane. The follow-up 'They don't let murderers be captains' / 'Come on, let's go' perfectly dismisses the objection and moves on. Eight on craft is justified: it's tight, surprising, and character-perfect.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'They don't let murderers be captains.' — delivered as a genuine refutation
jokes.setup: Bevers has just claimed the captain is a murderer
jokes.punchline: Abbi's response treats the absurd claim with straight-faced procedural logic, which is itself funny
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man beat. The comedy is in Abbi taking the absurdist claim seriously enough to refute it logically.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana exchange looks after 'The captain is a murderer' — implied beat of incredulous silence before following him anyway
jokes.setup: Bevers drops the captain-is-a-murderer bomb
jokes.punchline: The audience infers (from the scene gap and their eventual compliance) that Abbi and Ilana share a look of disbelief — and then follow him anyway, which is its own joke about their relationship with Bevers
jokes.explanation: Inferred visual beat. Their eventual willingness to follow Bevers after the murderer claim is funny in its own right — the lure of free liquor overrides all rational concerns.

jokes.text: They get locked in the storage room immediately after finding the liquor
jokes.setup: They finally found the liquor and are celebrating
jokes.punchline: The door locks instantly — the universe punishes their small victory immediately
jokes.explanation: Standard sitcom reversal — the luck turns immediately. Functional escalation setup, not particularly original.

jokes.text: Ilana yells 'Rape! Rape!' to get help through a locked door
jokes.setup: They're locked in a storage room and need to alert people outside
jokes.punchline: Ilana's go-to emergency signal is 'rape' — with complete conviction — prompting Abbi to correct her that you're supposed to yell 'fire'
jokes.explanation: This is very Ilana — her instinct goes immediately to the most extreme signifier of female distress as a practical tool rather than a panic response. Abbi's correction ('it's fire, you yell fire') treats it as a simple factual matter, which makes it funnier. Character integration is high: Ilana's whole persona is built on weaponizing feminist frameworks in unexpected contexts.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'Wait, wait, move out of the way. I'm gonna ram the door. The man is gonna ram the door. Wanna see me ram it?' — then immediately injures himself
jokes.setup: They need to break down the door and Bevers announces he'll handle it with great masculine ceremony
jokes.punchline: He hypes the ram with three separate announcements, then immediately hurts himself — the 'Ah! [bleep]' followed by moaning
jokes.explanation: The anti-hero physical comedy beat — Bevers' grandiose self-announcement vs. immediate failure. The escalating self-promotion ('The man is gonna ram the door. Wanna see me ram it?') before the pratfall is the structure. Not original but the Bevers voice makes it character-specific.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'No, it's fire, you yell fire.' — Ilana immediately: 'Oh, fine. Fire!'
jokes.setup: Abbi corrects Ilana's emergency yell protocol
jokes.punchline: Ilana accepts the correction instantly and pivots to 'fire!' without any apparent irony, implying she'll just use whatever word she's told
jokes.explanation: Good button on the previous joke. Ilana's zero-hesitation pivot is funny because she shows no residual attachment to her original wrong answer.

jokes.text: Ilana's evolving emergency chants: 'Fire! Fire and rape! Rape and fire! Fire rape!'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been corrected that 'fire' is the right emergency word, not 'rape'
jokes.punchline: Unable to commit to either correction or original instinct, she combines them in increasingly chaotic permutations — 'fire rape' as a final compound emergency
jokes.explanation: The escalation callback to joke 25 is brilliantly structured. Ilana can't choose between 'fire' and 'rape' as her emergency word so she starts combining them, reaching 'fire rape' as an absurdist portmanteau of distress signals. The progression ('Fire! Fire and rape! Rape and fire! Fire rape!') is a perfect escalation sequence — each step is funnier than the last and 'fire rape' as a compound emergency is quotable and bizarre. Character integration is high because only Ilana would approach this problem as one of word-ordering rather than realizing both are wrong in this context. Among the episode's best bits.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I honestly have always thought that we'd die in each other's arms at sea.'
jokes.setup: They're trapped in a storage room on a boat and the situation is looking bad
jokes.punchline: Ilana breaks from problem-solving to share a romantic fantasy about a sea death — completely sincere, as if this is a comforting thought
jokes.explanation: This is a perfect Ilana line — the genuine romanticism of imagining dying with your best friend at sea, delivered as if it's a comforting vision rather than a horrifying one. It captures the show's central theme (the depth of Abbi and Ilana's friendship) while being absurdly dark. Character integration is very high.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'Sorry, Abbi, I broke your Starbucks gift card.' / Abbi: 'Bevers, I still had $17 on that.'
jokes.setup: They're trapped, possibly dying, and Bevers apologizes for a broken Starbucks gift card
jokes.punchline: Abbi's immediate, wounded '$17' response — the mundane specificity of the amount and her genuine distress about it in a survival situation
jokes.explanation: A great broke-twentysomething joke. The specificity of '$17' is key — not $20, not 'some money,' but $17 remaining on a gift card, which she knew exactly. Abbi prioritizing the gift card financial loss in a potential emergency is both character-perfect and universally relatable to broke people. Bevers destroying it and offering a lecture about local businesses as consolation makes it a two-part escalation.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'Well, maybe now you can start supporting local businesses.' — after breaking Abbi's Starbucks gift card
jokes.setup: Abbi is upset about her $17 Starbucks balance being destroyed
jokes.punchline: Bevers offers unsolicited anti-corporate lifestyle advice as if he's done Abbi a favor — the man who squats in her apartment lecturing her about spending habits
jokes.explanation: The follow-up is as good as the setup. Bevers pivoting from apology to progressive consumer lecture is a perfect beat of his particular brand of oblivious entitlement — he lives rent-free in Abbi's apartment and is now advising her on ethical spending. The irony is entirely character-driven.

jokes.text: Bevers calculates bar restock timing: 'If each one of them has at least three drinks per hour, that's 750 drinks every hour... They're gonna have to restock the bar in 20 to 25 minutes.'
jokes.setup: They're locked in a storage room and Bevers is calculating when someone will open the door to restock the bar
jokes.punchline: Bevers — who is apparently an idiot — does rapid alcohol consumption math with perfect confidence, delivering a logistics plan as if he's a field general
jokes.explanation: Bevers being competent in specific, unexpected ways is a reliable character engine. His math is actually reasonable, which makes it funnier that this is his escape plan. Abbi's 'What is this, the 2400-point SAT?' is a good button.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'What is this, the 2400-point SAT?' after Bevers does drink math
jokes.setup: Bevers is doing unexpectedly precise math in a crisis
jokes.punchline: Abbi's '2400-point SAT' retort — implying the math is both impressive and completely the wrong use of intelligence in this moment
jokes.explanation: Solid retort, slightly dated (2400-point SAT is now an older reference) but the rhythm of the line works. Functional Abbi deadpan.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I'm gonna be wearing my American flag contact lenses. Yeah, they burn me very badly. I should stop.'
jokes.setup: Discussing attire for Jaime's naturalization ceremony
jokes.punchline: Ilana has American flag contact lenses (already absurd), AND they hurt her, AND she announces she probably should stop wearing them — all three facts delivered as a casual aside
jokes.explanation: The structure here is excellent: the existence of the patriotic contact lenses is funny; that they cause her physical pain is funnier; that she knows she should stop wearing them but doesn't commits to the character. Only Ilana owns novelty patriotic contacts that burn her corneas. The self-awareness followed by continued use is perfect Ilana.

jokes.text: Lincoln pranks Jaime: 'Hey, cheers to you for becoming a citizen, and also to me — this is my first time on a boat.' / Jaime: 'No, prank, man. I'm a well-to-do dentist, of course I've been on a boat before.'
jokes.setup: After Jaime's earlier prank about failing citizenship, Lincoln claims this is his first boat ride
jokes.punchline: Jaime immediately prank-reverses it — he's a 'well-to-do dentist,' of course he's been on boats. The episode's prank structure echoing back.
jokes.explanation: The prank-calling episode callback works well — Jaime has established himself as a prankster in the cold open, so his instant deflation of Lincoln's emotional claim with 'prank, man' is satisfying. 'Well-to-do dentist' is a funny self-description. The structure mirrors the cold open nicely.

jokes.text: Jaime's elaborate fake Latin American cheers ceremony: 'That is how we cheers in Latin America.' / Lincoln: 'Really?' / Jaime: 'No, man, prank, bitch.'
jokes.setup: After the boat-prank exchange, Jaime performs an elaborate cheers gesture
jokes.punchline: Claims it's authentic Latin American tradition — it's not, it's another prank. 'Prank, bitch' as a sign-off.
jokes.explanation: The running prank structure is working well at this point. The cultural authority play — claiming something is a Latin tradition so the white American has to accept it — has an extra satirical layer. 'Prank, bitch' as Jaime's catchphrase sign-off is genuinely quotable and the best iteration of the running gag. Lincoln's 'I'm stealin' that one' is a nice button.

jokes.text: 'Kill, fuck, marry' with Captain Morgan, Jack Daniels, Svedka vodka, and... robot
jokes.setup: Abbi starts a 'kill/fuck/marry' game using liquor brand mascots
jokes.punchline: The list of three expected masculine mascots is derailed by the inclusion of 'robot' as the fourth option
jokes.explanation: The game itself is setup for the robot marriage joke that follows. Functional absurdist escalation — including a robot alongside liquor mascots is funny precisely because it accepts the premise completely.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Easy — kill the dudes, fuck and marry that robot.' — without hesitation
jokes.setup: Four options: Captain Morgan, Jack Daniels, Svedka robot, mystery robot
jokes.punchline: Ilana dismisses all three human mascots ('kill the dudes') to marry and fuck the robot — delivered with total certainty, as if there's no possible other answer
jokes.explanation: The certainty is the joke — there's no deliberation, no 'hmm,' just 'kill the dudes.' Ilana's complete indifference to conventional masculine appeal in favor of a robot is character-perfect. The follow-up civil rights bit makes this even better.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'You guys know that human-robot marriage is gonna be the civil rights issue of our lifetime?'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just said she'd fuck and marry the robot
jokes.punchline: She extends her robot attraction into a sincerely held political conviction — human-robot marriage as civil rights issue — delivered with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. The escalation from 'I'd marry the robot' to 'this is the civil rights issue of our lifetime' is a perfect Ilana move — she takes a personal absurd preference and immediately inflates it to political principle. The character integration is exceptional: Ilana's persona is built on earnest progressive activism, so her applying that same moral gravity to robot marriage is the funniest possible extension of who she is. It's quotable, surprising, and has a slight truth to it (AI/robot rights discourse does exist). The [08:09] reaction beat after — presumably a silent look from the others — is part of the joke. Justified 8+ because it's genuinely original, the structure escalates perfectly, and it could only come from Ilana.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'Would you rather drink a cup of mayonnaise or a cup of jazz?' — introducing 'jazz' as a euphemism
jokes.setup: Would you rather game, Bevers' turn — seemingly a gross food option vs. something benign
jokes.punchline: Jazz is not benign — it's semen. The reveal that mayonnaise was the LESS disgusting option.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection is well-constructed. 'Jazz' sounds euphemistic but not necessarily dirty — you're prepared for something weird but the reveal is perfectly calibrated. The setup implies mayonnaise is the gross option, inverting the expected grossness. Bevers introducing his own semen slang at a formal lawyer boat party is character-appropriate: he's a man with a personal vocabulary for his own ejaculate.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'What the fuck is jazz?' — after Bevers introduces the term
jokes.setup: Bevers asks about drinking 'a cup of jazz'
jokes.punchline: Ilana's completely legitimate bafflement — 'What the fuck is jazz?' — sets up Bevers' definition perfectly
jokes.explanation: Functional straight-man setup line. Does its job of triggering the explanation without adding much itself.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'I like to call it jazz because it comes out of my horn and you never know where it's gonna go.'
jokes.setup: Bevers defines 'jazz' as semen, then explains his reasoning
jokes.punchline: The jazz metaphor is actually worked out with genuine internal logic: horn instrument, improvisation, unpredictability. The joke is that Bevers has clearly thought about this at length.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's strongest jokes. The metaphor ('it comes out of my horn and you never know where it's gonna go') is genuinely clever — the jazz analogy actually works on multiple levels (improvisation, horns, unpredictability) which makes it funnier than if it were just gross slang. The joke rewards the audience for knowing jazz as a musical form. The craft is real: Bevers has constructed a surprisingly coherent conceptual metaphor for something disgusting, which is funnier than simple crudeness would be. High quotability because the line can be deployed outside context. Justified 8+ because the wordplay is original, the structure of the extended metaphor works, and it's deeply character-specific — Bevers has a NAME for this.

jokes.text: Bevers' Patois accent: 'Me hearing a lot of chitchat. Me don't hear you answering that question, why?'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are refusing to answer the mayonnaise-or-jazz question
jokes.punchline: Bevers adopts a Caribbean patois voice to pressure them, with absolutely no explanation or setup for why
jokes.explanation: The unexplained accent pivot is Bevers at his most unhinged. It works because there's no justification — he just decides to apply pressure in patois. The cringe comes from the total non-sequitur of it. The quality depends heavily on performance.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I would chug the jazz.' — after refusing to answer, finally answering
jokes.setup: Abbi has refused and the group has been trying to dodge the question
jokes.punchline: Ilana answers with complete seriousness and commitment — not just drink it, CHUG it
jokes.explanation: 'I would chug the jazz' is four words that do enormous work. The escalation from 'drink' to 'chug,' the absolute commitment, and the context all combine for a high-economy joke. Pure Ilana: transgressive, fearless, and specific. The chug is the key upgrade — it's not just a gross answer, it's an enthusiastic one.

jokes.text: Jaime (American accent): 'I love hamburgers... and I love DUI's.'
jokes.setup: Jaime is practicing an American accent on Lincoln's encouragement. He starts with an expected American cliché (hamburgers).
jokes.punchline: He pairs the most wholesome American signifier (hamburgers) with its dark counterpart (DUIs) as equally iconic American experiences
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The construction is perfect: 'I love hamburgers' sets up a wholesome Americana bit, so 'and I love DUI's' lands with maximum force. The pairing argues that DUIs are just as essentially American as hamburgers — an absurd claim that also works as social commentary. The delivery in American accent makes it funnier because it captures a specific type of performative American bro-dom. Jaime's outsider perspective on American culture makes this funnier and sharper than if any other character said it. High quotability, strong character integration. Justified 8+ because the misdirection is clean, the claim is insightful-absurd, and economy is excellent.

jokes.text: The Michigan State Delta Sig guy immediately recognizes Jaime's accent as 'one of us'
jokes.setup: Jaime is doing his best fake American accent, which includes 'I love DUI's'
jokes.punchline: A frat guy immediately claims him as a fellow Delta Sig from Michigan State — Jaime's caricature of American bro culture is indistinguishable from the real thing
jokes.explanation: The callback confirmation that Jaime's American parody IS the American experience. The joke is that he's accepted as real because his performance is accurate. 'That's correct, bro-sef, I was a frat bro upon the campus' is a great line — 'upon the campus' is the tell that reveals the performance, but the frat guy doesn't notice.

jokes.text: Jaime: 'That's correct, bro-sef. I was a frat bro upon the campus.'
jokes.setup: The frat guy has accepted Jaime as a fellow Delta Sig
jokes.punchline: 'Upon the campus' — the overly formal phrasing that reveals the non-native English speaker playing American, which the frat guy completely misses
jokes.explanation: 'Upon the campus' is the episode's most precise piece of writing in this sequence. The 'bro-sef' is expected; 'upon the campus' is the tell that nobody notices. It's the kind of phrasing a very intelligent non-native speaker might use when improvising in a second language — technically correct but rhythmically foreign. The frat guy's complete obliviousness to the tell is what makes Jaime's performance commentary so sharp.

jokes.text: Abbi eating food off a surface without asking what it is — '[Abbi], I don't think anybody washed those.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is hungry and starts eating something off a surface in what is presumably still the storage room
jokes.punchline: She's told the things weren't washed — she doesn't care: 'I don't even care, I am so hungry right now.'
jokes.explanation: Competent visual/reaction beat. Abbi's hunger overriding hygiene concerns is relatable-broke-person comedy but not especially crafted.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'Because the shrimp is always the first thing to get eaten on these boats.' — completely reasonable explanation for pocket shrimp
jokes.setup: Abbi asks why he has shrimp in his pocket
jokes.punchline: His explanation is actually logical — he pre-pockets shrimp because it goes fast. The horror is not the logic but the commitment to this strategy.
jokes.explanation: The pocket shrimp explanation is funnier than the reveal because it's correct. Bevers has genuinely solved the problem of shrimp scarcity at events. The connection to Ilana's earlier 'I brought ziplocs' makes it a callback that deepens both jokes. Two people independently strategizing about boat food acquisition is a perfect character parallel. Bevers' competence in this specific arena, consistent with his bar math, establishes him as a man with a very particular skill set. Justified near-8 on craft.

jokes.text: Ilana's 'And she is occupato' while holding up her ziploc — presumably already filled
jokes.setup: Bevers notes Ilana got a ziploc that morning
jokes.punchline: Ilana has already filled hers — presumably also with stolen boat food — and declares it occupied with an Italian flair
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the opening ziploc distribution — Ilana's forethought paid off, and she's using it unashamedly. 'Occupato' as a word choice is a characteristically Ilana unnecessary Italian flourish.

jokes.text: Bevers reveals the ring and vocalizes his own dramatic fanfare: 'Bom-bom bwooom.'
jokes.setup: Bevers shows a ring — he's going to propose to Melody tonight
jokes.punchline: He provides his own sound effects for the dramatic reveal, performing his own cinema score
jokes.explanation: Bevers narrating his own dramatic moments with self-supplied sound effects is deeply character-specific. The 'bom-bom bwooom' is performed with total commitment. Sets up the proposal arc while being funny in itself.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I really haven't thought about it, but I'd be Posh Spice.' — instantly in a Spice Girls debate
jokes.setup: The conversation has somehow shifted to which Spice Girl each person would be (presumably as a tangent from the proposal)
jokes.punchline: The non-sequitur subject change to Spice Girls is the joke, plus Ilana claiming not to have thought about it while immediately having a confident answer
jokes.explanation: Competent Ilana character comedy. The 'I really haven't thought about it' followed immediately by a confident answer is a good self-awareness joke but not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: Jaime: 'I wish there could've been like, a Latina one called the Spicy Spice, but this is just a fantasy of mine.'
jokes.setup: Spice Girls assignment discussion — Jaime can't find himself in the group
jokes.punchline: Jaime's gentle lamentation that Spice Girls representation didn't include a Latina — delivered as a polite personal wish rather than a critique
jokes.explanation: The 'but this is just a fantasy of mine' delivery makes it work — Jaime's resigned acceptance of his non-representation, treated as a minor personal daydream, captures something real about being a Latino man trying to find himself in pop culture. Character-specific and quietly pointed.

jokes.text: Lincoln: 'Open your eyes, man. Shakira is Spicy Spice.' / Ilana: 'Oh my God. You're a genius.'
jokes.setup: Jaime laments the nonexistence of Spicy Spice
jokes.punchline: Lincoln solves the problem by retroactively assigning Shakira as the missing Spice Girl — with total authority — and Ilana treats this as a revelation
jokes.explanation: Lincoln's confident Shakira declaration and Ilana's genuine awe work together well. The idea that this is new and brilliant information is the joke — Shakira as Spicy Spice is so obvious it's funny that it's treated as a discovery. 'You're a genius' from Ilana to Lincoln is a nice small character moment.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'Besides, we already live together.' / Abbi: 'No, you do not live together. You have your own apartment.' / Bevers: 'Jesus!'
jokes.setup: Bevers is rationalizing proposing to Melody — 'we already live together'
jokes.punchline: Abbi points out that he does NOT live with Melody — he squats in Abbi's apartment. This is the central irony of Bevers' entire existence on the show. His genuine shock at the correction is the cap.
jokes.explanation: This is peak Bevers mythology. The show's entire running joke about Bevers' parasitic cohabitation reaches a perfect moment: Bevers has so thoroughly convinced himself that he lives with Melody that he's using it as a marriage argument. Abbi's correction and Bevers' 'Jesus!' — as if she's the unreasonable one — is exactly right. Character integration is near-perfect because this joke requires the full backstory of Bevers to land. Justified 9 on character integration.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Does this mean that you'd be moving out?' — with clear emotional investment in the answer
jokes.setup: Bevers potentially proposing and getting married means he might finally leave Abbi's apartment
jokes.punchline: Abbi's hope is barely contained — the proposal subplot is not romantic to her, it's a potential housing solution
jokes.explanation: The subtext is the joke — Abbi is rooting for the proposal as eviction strategy. Her question is framed neutrally but the hope is obvious. Character integration is high because this is Abbi's entire relationship with Bevers distilled: she's not rooting against his happiness, she's rooting for her apartment.

jokes.text: Bevers asks Abbi to be his best man. Abbi's reaction: 'Bevers, you have four brothers.'
jokes.setup: Bevers asks Abbi to be his best man at the wedding
jokes.punchline: Abbi points out the obvious: he has four brothers. Who presumably exist for this exact function.
jokes.explanation: Bevers preferring the woman who barely tolerates him to his own four brothers as best man is both deeply pathetic and genuinely funny. Abbi's flat citation of the brothers is the right response — she's not touched, she's confused. Bevers' follow-up ('I feel like you're the one who really gets me') is the devastating cap.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'When we get out of here, I'm gonna pop the big Q in front of everybody... I already worked it out with the DJ. I'm such a showboat.'
jokes.setup: Bevers plans his proposal logistics
jokes.punchline: 'I'm such a showboat' — on a boat — delivered without apparent awareness of the pun, which makes it funnier
jokes.explanation: The accidental (or possibly intentional) pun 'showboat' on a boat is a gentle wordplay moment. Also 'the big Q' for proposal/question is mildly funny Bevers-speak. The DJ alliance confirms Bevers has done elaborate preparation for something he's also apparently completely unprepared for emotionally.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Government-mandated monogamy is for old people, like Facebook invites or network TV.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is arguing against marriage for young people
jokes.punchline: The comparison class — Facebook invites, network TV — places institutional monogamy in a very specific category of things that are technically functional but that young people have moved past
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best written lines. The comparison is precise and right — Facebook invites and network TV are both things that exist, that older people use, that feel vaguely obligatory, and that millennials have structurally abandoned without formal declaration. Applying the same framework to monogamy is sharp social observation wrapped in Ilana's particular brand of unsolicited political declaration. 'Government-mandated monogamy' as a framing is itself funny because it treats marriage as regulatory compliance. Highly quotable, character-perfect. Justified 8+ because the comparison class is original and sharp, not just a vague anti-marriage opinion.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Or it's not, Ilana.' — flat counter to the government-mandated monogamy speech
jokes.setup: Ilana has delivered an elaborate anti-marriage philosophy
jokes.punchline: Abbi's complete dismissal in four words — not engaging with the argument, just refusing it
jokes.explanation: 'Or it's not, Ilana' is exceptional economy. The comma before 'Ilana' carries significant weight — it's the weary punctuation of someone who has heard too many Ilana declarations. Does exactly what it needs to in four syllables.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'We're sex friends... although we haven't had sex in like, four days. You know, today I was actually with him all day, but I didn't see his dick once.'
jokes.setup: Jaime asks about Ilana and Lincoln's relationship status
jokes.punchline: Ilana's definition of 'sex friends' immediately undermines itself — no sex for four days, and the day's measure of intimacy is dick visibility. The non-sequitur metric ('I didn't see his dick once') reframes the entire relationship.
jokes.explanation: Deeply character-specific. Ilana's relationship barometer being 'did I see his penis today' is a perfect expression of how she experiences intimacy — as explicitly physical, and the absence of the physical signals something is wrong. The line 'I didn't see his dick once' as an indicator of emotional distance is both genuinely funny and a real characterization beat. High character integration because no other character on television could deliver this observation about their own relationship.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'Melody and I haven't had sex in weeks, on purpose though. It's called edging.'
jokes.setup: The conversation about who's having sex
jokes.punchline: Bevers volunteers his sex life details and misapplies 'edging' — which is an individual technique for prolonged arousal — to a weeks-long relationship dry spell
jokes.explanation: Bevers oversharing is always funny; Bevers misapplying sex terminology to reframe a failure as a strategy is his particular gift. The 'on purpose though' is doing a lot of self-deceiving work.

jokes.text: Bevers explains edging via whale blowhole analogy: 'You know how whales have that blowhole?'
jokes.setup: Bevers is explaining the concept of edging to Jaime
jokes.punchline: He uses the whale blowhole as his explanatory model for human orgasm — completely sincere, as if this is standard pedagogy
jokes.explanation: Bevers' choice of the blowhole as an explanatory device is characteristically unhinged. The show's aquatic setting makes the whale reference funnier — he has context. Jaime's 'Yeah, o... okay' as a non-committal response is the right beat.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I'm a sexual X-man, I'm Wolverine. I'm Vulva-rine.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is insisting she's wild and free and not in a relationship
jokes.punchline: 'Vulva-rine' — a self-coined superhero name that merges anatomy with Wolverine, delivered with complete earnestness as a statement of identity
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. The escalation from 'I'm a sexual X-Man' to 'I'm Wolverine' to 'I'm Vulva-rine' is a perfect three-step. The portmanteau works on multiple levels: Wolverine's powers (retractable claws, aggressive self-protection, regeneration) map hilariously onto Ilana's sexual persona. The self-naming as 'Vulva-rine' is the kind of thing only Ilana would do, and she means it completely. High character integration and strong economy.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'Me thinks the lady doth protest too much.' — in a terrible British accent
jokes.setup: Ilana is screaming about how wild and free she is after everyone implies she has feelings for Lincoln
jokes.punchline: Bevers — the least likely Shakespeare quoter — calmly delivers the apt literary reference in a fake British accent, and he's completely right
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: Bevers producing an apt Shakespeare reference is unexpected and therefore funny, and the British accent affectation makes it funnier. It's also functionally correct analysis, which is even more deflating for Ilana. Bevers landing the most accurate observation in the episode is a good character subversion.

jokes.text: 'It smells like balls in here.' — upon being freed from the storage room
jokes.setup: Three people have been trapped in a ship storage room for a significant time
jokes.punchline: The rescuer's immediate sensory observation — not 'are you okay?' but a frank olfactory assessment
jokes.explanation: Functional visual/olfactory gag. The balls comment earns a laugh as a release valve after the storage room sequence but isn't particularly crafted — it's a predictable gross-out capper.

jokes.text: Jaime screaming 'My insulin!' as he runs to them
jokes.setup: The group has been freed from the storage room after an extended confinement
jokes.punchline: Jaime's first concern upon reunion is his insulin — implying he or someone he loves needed it urgently, played as physical comedy as he runs toward them
jokes.explanation: Odd, quick beat. The insulin emergency escalates briefly the stakes while being played for comedy. Not fully developed but the specificity of 'insulin' is funnier than a generic medical concern.

jokes.text: Ilana confessional: 'If Lincoln and I are a couple, it's like, why did my ancestors even come here?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is processing whether she and Lincoln are accidentally a couple
jokes.punchline: She connects the existential threat of monogamy to her family's entire immigration narrative — having a boyfriend is the thing that nullifies the American dream for her
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and among the best in the series. The structure is elegant: Ilana places 'having a boyfriend' in the same existential category as 'the reason my family immigrated to America.' It's absurdist but also has a genuine internal Ilana logic — freedom, especially sexual freedom, is her primary value, and compromising it is an existential failure. The callback to the episode's citizenship/immigration theme adds resonance. 'Why did my ancestors even come here?' is perfectly structured as a serious immigration question that reveals a completely trivial concern. Near-9 on character integration because the joke requires the full Ilana worldview. The economy is exceptional — one sentence does the work of a paragraph. Justified 8.5+ on craft.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I don't know.' — responding to 'why did my ancestors even come here'
jokes.setup: Ilana has asked why her ancestors came to America if she can't be sexually free
jokes.punchline: Abbi responds as if the question has a real answer she's just not sure about
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Abbi doesn't question the framing at all — she's genuinely trying to think of an answer to an unanswerable question. Her straight-man accommodation of Ilana's insane logic is the episode's central comedic relationship.

jokes.text: Abbi offers pragmatic solution: 'Why don't you just have sex with Lincoln? And then you're back to being friends, right?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is spiraling about whether she and Lincoln are accidentally a couple
jokes.punchline: Abbi treats sex as a reset button on relationship status — clinically practical advice that has its own inverted logic
jokes.explanation: Good Abbi-as-rational-friend beat. She's genuinely trying to help and her logic, while absurd, follows from what Ilana has established about how their 'friendship' works. The 'right?' at the end is perfect — she's not sure this works either.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'You got beauty, you got brains, you're a fucking genius.' — to Abbi, right before being distracted
jokes.setup: Abbi has just offered the sex-as-friend-reset solution
jokes.punchline: Ilana lavishes Abbi with excessive flattery for what is objectively a somewhat confused idea, then immediately gets distracted by Lincoln and Jaime arriving
jokes.explanation: Functional affection beat between Abbi and Ilana. The excess of the compliment is funny but not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'The boys with the penises!' — on seeing Lincoln and Jaime
jokes.setup: Lincoln and Jaime arrive looking for the women
jokes.punchline: Ilana greets them with an anatomical category description rather than their names — delivered with genuine delight
jokes.explanation: The 'boys with the penises' framing is Ilana's particular way of being delighted — her relationship to anatomy as category rather than person is consistent characterization.

jokes.text: Lincoln: 'We were looking everywhere for you. Except, we forgot to look for you.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln and Jaime have apparently not found Abbi and Ilana despite looking
jokes.punchline: Lincoln adds the correction as if it's a neutral clarification — they forgot to actually look — delivered without embarrassment
jokes.explanation: The self-correction is perfectly structured — 'looking everywhere' followed by 'except we forgot to look' is a classic reversal that Lincoln delivers as simple correction rather than apology. The economy is strong. One of Lincoln's better lines.

jokes.text: Jaime asking if either of them has a knife 'because me and Lincoln want to become blood brothers'
jokes.setup: Jaime and Lincoln are on a citizenship-celebration cruise together
jokes.punchline: Jaime wants to formalize the friendship with a blood oath — a completely earnest request in a formal lawyer setting, asking women to produce a knife
jokes.explanation: Endearing absurdism. Jaime treating the blood brothers ritual as a normal request is consistent with his character's earnest enthusiasm. The incongruity of asking women at a fancy law party for a weapon for friendship purposes is the joke.

jokes.text: Ilana proposing 'P to P' to Lincoln — 'Pubes to Pubes'
jokes.setup: After establishing she and Lincoln haven't had sex and need to resolve their relationship status
jokes.punchline: 'P to P — Pubes to Pubes' as her approach to the sex reset, immediately dragging Lincoln off
jokes.explanation: Ilana executing Abbi's plan immediately and with her own coined terminology is perfect. 'P to P — Pubes to Pubes' as a sex initiation phrase is both disgusting and funny, and Lincoln's 'That's a weird way to say that, but...' while going along with it is the right button. The callback to Abbi's advice landing this fast is a good structural payoff.

jokes.text: Abbi and Jaime find someone having sex in the bathroom and announce 'Hey! There are people fucking in here! Rude!'
jokes.setup: Abbi needs to use the bathroom
jokes.punchline: The bathroom is occupied by sex-havers; Abbi's response is indignation at the rudeness rather than embarrassment at the interruption — 'Rude!'
jokes.explanation: Functional physical comedy. The 'rude' response is the joke — Abbi treating public sex as a social etiquette violation rather than anything else. Not particularly original.

jokes.text: The sex sounds continuing audibly followed by the man casually emerging: 'Oh, hey, what's up, man? I remember you from earlier. He's a nice guy.'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Jaime interrupt bathroom sex
jokes.punchline: The man emerges from the bathroom mid-sex to offer a friendly greeting and character endorsement of the other guy still inside, completely unbothered
jokes.explanation: 'He's a nice guy' as a bathroom sex character reference is funny. The contrast between the context and the normal-guy-at-a-party conversational register is the joke.

jokes.text: The PA klaxon and 'We're going down' fake-out — 'I'm kidding! You all know what that sound means... it's a love emergency!'
jokes.setup: Emergency klaxon and announcement that they're 'going down' — panic implied
jokes.punchline: The DJ immediately undercuts it: 'I'm kidding! It's a love emergency!' — the Bevers proposal setup
jokes.explanation: Light misdirection that serves a plot function. Functional but not particularly crafted.

jokes.text: Abbi to Bevers before the mic: 'Yes, you can, you can marry Melody and you can build a life that is completely outside of my apartment.'
jokes.setup: Bevers is scared to go up to the mic and propose
jokes.punchline: Abbi's pep talk is entirely self-interested — she's encouraging him to propose specifically so he'll move out. Her enthusiasm is direct proportional to her desire for her apartment back.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Abbi's housing motivation makes this pep talk genuinely funny. 'A life completely outside of my apartment' is a beautifully specific framing of romantic encouragement as eviction planning. Character integration is high — this is Abbi as a fundamentally decent person whose decency is being weaponized for self-interest.

jokes.text: Bevers' proposal speech goes off the rails: 'Uh, yeah, so Abbi... [pause] ...is my best man.'
jokes.setup: Bevers is at the mic to propose to Melody in front of 250 lawyers
jokes.punchline: Instead of proposing, he begins listing features of the boat ('we're on a boat... on the water, nice facilities, great crew') and then pivots to announcing Abbi's best man status — completely forgetting to propose
jokes.explanation: The Bevers proposal implosion is a sustained cringe sequence. Narrating the boat's features as though establishing context for something he never arrives at is perfectly Bevers. The 'nice facilities, great crew' is the key beat — he's doing a Yelp review at his own proposal. The hard pivot to Abbi as best man is a great anti-climax.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'Abbi's transitioning into becoming a man, and I'm just so proud of her.'
jokes.setup: Bevers needs Abbi to speak at the mic to somehow rescue the situation
jokes.punchline: He announces — to 250 lawyers at Melody's firm — that Abbi is transitioning, apparently as a way to hand off the microphone
jokes.explanation: Bevers announcing Abbi's gender transition as a crowd-warming strategy is characteristically unhinged and has a great double layer: he has zero reason to say this, it makes things worse, and 'I'm just so proud of her' is the perfectly earnest Bevers touch. The crowd's cheering response adds to the absurdity.

jokes.text: Abbi at the mic, after the crowd cheers her transition announcement: 'Okay, yeah... It's true. Uh... I'm becoming a man! I am becoming... a man!'
jokes.setup: Bevers has announced Abbi is transitioning and handed her the mic, expecting her to fix things
jokes.punchline: Abbi — horrified but socially unable to contradict the momentum — commits to the transition announcement in front of 250 lawyers at Melody's firm
jokes.explanation: Abbi's capitulation to the crowd's expectation is one of the episode's funniest character beats. The timestamps show her taking a genuinely long beat — 16:50 to 16:59, nine seconds of processing — before committing with 'Okay, yeah... It's true.' The escalation to 'I AM BECOMING... A MAN!' is Abbi's people-pleasing taken to its natural conclusion: she would rather publicly announce a fictional gender transition than disappoint 250 strangers. This is deep Abbi character comedy. The pauses in the transcript are the joke — her process of arriving at the decision.

jokes.text: Implication: 'All the lawyers are now into me.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has announced to a room of lawyers that she is transitioning
jokes.punchline: The unexpected benefit: the law firm crowd is apparently pro-transition in the most supportive way — they are now attracted to her. Callbacks to Lincoln's earlier speculation about lawyers and trans women.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Lincoln's 'lawyers love trans women' observation pays off perfectly — he was right. The joke's structure is a long-delayed setup-punchline. Abbi's neutral reporting of the situation ('all the lawyers are now into me') is funny precisely because she's not sure if this is good or bad.

jokes.text: Abbi reports back: 'All the lawyers are now into me.' — deadpan, after her transgender announcement
jokes.setup: Bevers asks Abbi to go talk to Melody; Abbi explains what happened after the announcement
jokes.punchline: A callback to the 'Denises the penises / lawyers love 'em' joke from earlier — the lawyers' attraction to Abbi, now that they believe she's trans, confirms Lincoln's absurdist theory
jokes.explanation: A superb callback that completes Lincoln's earlier absurdist theory. 'All the lawyers are now into me' delivered with flat exhaustion is both the payoff of joke #9 and a perfect Abbi reaction beat. The economy is exceptional — five words do the work of a whole scene. The callback structure elevates this well above a standalone joke.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'Because of you. You and Ilana made me realize we shouldn't be getting married yet. We're too young.' — then: 'So, you told everyone I'm transitioning into becoming a man.'
jokes.setup: Abbi confronts Bevers about why he didn't propose
jokes.punchline: He blames Abbi and Ilana's anti-marriage speech — then Abbi calmly points out the consequence: he told 250 people she's transitioning
jokes.explanation: The logical chain — Ilana's speech influenced Bevers' non-proposal which caused Abbi's fake transition — is a satisfying narrative callback. Bevers using Ilana's progressive philosophy to justify his failure while accidentally outing Abbi as something she isn't is peak Bevers chaos.

jokes.text: Abbi's speech to Melody through bathroom door goes on — then she pivots mid-speech to: 'Like, listen, I think we need to actually schedule a time to talk about that, just the two of us, 'cause he just eats all of my stuff.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is delivering a heartfelt speech through the bathroom door to Melody about Bevers's love
jokes.punchline: She derails her own romantic advocacy to schedule a grievance meeting about Bevers eating her food — her real complaints bleeding out mid-speech
jokes.explanation: Another callback to Abbi's Bevers grievances bleeding into otherwise sincere moments. The 'he just eats all of my stuff' as a mid-advocacy derailment is a great character joke — Abbi can't separate her genuine warmth toward Bevers from her very real food-theft resentment.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'Well, somebody must've sweet-talked her, 'cause we just boned in a lifeboat. There was so much jazz.'
jokes.setup: Abbi says she never talked to Melody — yet Melody has apparently reconciled with Bevers
jokes.punchline: Bevers reveals the reconciliation was consummated in a lifeboat, using his established jazz euphemism for semen
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff to the jazz/blowhole explanation from the locked room. 'There was so much jazz' in a lifeboat is a perfectly disgusting image delivered with Bevers's characteristic casual pride. The reuse of the euphemism earns its own laugh by now being an established vocabulary. The lifeboat location is a bonus absurdity.

jokes.text: Lincoln: 'Ow, my dick. It's too romantic, I'm sorry. Ah, we just had sex. It's still sore. I'm in my refractory period, you gotta let me rebuild.'
jokes.setup: Romantic music swells, the Statue of Liberty passes — an emotional moment
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's response to the romantic setting is to complain about genital soreness from recent sex — his refractory period as a counterpoint to a nationalistic/romantic crescendo
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the bathroom sex scene earlier. The comedy is in the juxtaposition: swelling orchestra, the Statue of Liberty, the culmination of an immigrant citizenship story — set against Lincoln explaining his refractory period. 'You gotta let me rebuild' is a wonderfully clinical way to talk about sex recovery at an emotionally elevated moment.

jokes.text: Lincoln: 'I'm sorry, I'm too hung over. My eyes can't focus on a fixed point right now.'
jokes.setup: Someone shows Lincoln something on the phone at the climactic citizenship ceremony moment
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's medical description of his hangover symptom ('can't focus on a fixed point') at a moment of national/emotional significance is a perfect bathetic response
jokes.explanation: Light deadpan. Lincoln's clinical self-diagnosis is consistent character voice. Works as a tonal counterpoint to the emotional ceremony.
